name: ginkgo biloba
mechanism: Proposed mechanism: Ginkgo extracts contain terpene lactones such as ginkgolide A; ginkgolide A is described as a platelet-activating factor antagonist, which may relate to bleeding concerns rather than proving broad therapeutic benefit.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
safetyNotes: Moderate-dose leaf extracts are likely safe for most adults and have been used up to 6 years in research, but seeds are toxic; adverse effects can include dizziness, GI symptoms, and headache; bleeding risk and pregnancy cautions apply.

sources.title: Herb-Drug Interactions: What the Science Says (Ginkgo section)
The Ginkgo Evaluation of Memory (GEM) Study

description: A very ancient deciduous tree; in modern supplement use, leaf extract is most common; seeds can be toxic if consumed.
dosage: 240 mg/day was used in the GEM trial evaluating dementia prevention.
duration: No direct duration data. Contextual inference: ; nan; nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference: ; nan; nan.. ; nan; nan. No direct effects data. interactions: Warfarin is associated with increased risk of major bleeding events; concomitant use with efavirenz is not recommended.
preparation: Typically leaf extract supplements; crude plant/seeds are not equivalent and seeds carry toxicity risk.
therapeuticUses: Used for memory loss, dementia, and circulatory issues
summary: Ginkgo leaf extract is widely marketed for cognitive and other claims, but there is no conclusive evidence of benefit for any condition, and the GEM trial found 240 mg/day ineffective for reducing dementia/Alzheimer’s development in older adults. Safety cautions include bleeding risk, potential drug interactions including warfarin and efavirenz, and toxicity of seeds.
